key_points: Climate refers to the average weather conditions of a place over a long period of time
India has a monsoon type of climate characterized by seasonal reversal of winds
The main elements of weather include temperature, pressure, wind, humidity and precipitation
Climatic controls that influence climate include latitude, altitude, pressure and wind systems, distance from sea, ocean currents and relief
The Himalayas protect India from cold winds coming from Central Asia
Temperature decreases with increase in altitude which explains why hill stations are cooler
The hot weather season occurs from March to May with high temperatures and hot winds called loo
The advancing monsoon season begins in June and brings rainfall to most parts of India
The retreating monsoon season occurs during October and November when monsoon winds withdraw
Rainfall distribution in India is uneven with heavy rainfall in northeastern India and low rainfall in Rajasthan and Ladakh
examples: Highest rainfall example: Mawsynram in Meghalaya
Example of winter rainfall: Western disturbances affecting northern India
Example of pre-monsoon rain: Mango showers in Kerala and Karnataka
Example of hot winds: Loo winds in northern plains
formulas: Climate = Average weather conditions over a long period
Continentality = Extreme climate in areas far from the sea
Orographic rainfall = Rainfall caused when moist air rises over mountains
exam_tips: Understand the difference between weather and climate
Remember the climatic controls influencing India's climate
Learn the four seasons of India and their characteristics
Focus on examples like Mawsynram and Ladakh when studying rainfall distribution
Understand the role of monsoon in Indian agriculture
